headlines.title: Thousands pack downtown San Jose for Colombia vs. Uzbekistan World Cup match
headlines.description: Thousands of Colombian fans packed San Pedro Square for the World Cup match between Uzbekistan and Colombia Wednesday, and even more are expected for Thursday’s match between Mexico and South Korea. World Cup was fever on full display in San Jose’s San Pedro Square. Colombian fans erupted in cheers after Colombia scored its first goal against Uzbekistan. “It’s my first time here in San Pedro Square, I love the energy, I love how all the community gets together to celebrate, and we can really enjoy safely,” said Camila, who was supporting Colombia. Security was tight as fans celebrated. Most people were supporting the South America country, but there were some also cheering on Uzbekistan. “It’s our first time in the World Cup, and I’m extremely happy,” Anastasia said. Even bigger crowds are expected Thursday night for the Mexico-South Korea watch party. San Pedro Square got a taste of just how passionate Mexican soccer fans are last Thursday. “As far as official numbers, let’s just say there wasn’t a square inch that wasn’t covered, well over 12,000 here in the plaza, so we’re expecting even more because this is an even bigger game,” San Jose Sports Authority Executive Director John Poach said. The city says there will be an increased police presence and more private security at Thursday’s event. There are also contingency plans to manage the overflow crowds, and make sure everyone can get a glimpse of the game. “We have San Pedro Square, and we have the next street to it open up. We’re also planning to open up Sharks Way and put up more screens there and have another additional overflow-screen area as well. And if the crowd gets even bigger, there are also plans to put additional screens walking distance from San Pedro Square,” 2026 San Jose Sports and Special Events Director Tommy O’Hare said. Restaurants and bars are also gearing up for all the fans. “Really stocking up, blew through much more than we anticipated, making sure that our staff is kept fresh, rotating them in, we’re fortunate that we have other stores that we can pull from,” Sushi Confidential VP of Operations Douglas Cookerly said. “Last week that Mexico was here, we didn’t really think that the crowd was going to be that much, so this week we’re well prepared, we’re staffing up, we’re prepping up the bar and kitchen,” Must Be Thai & Bar Owner Thalia Swangchaeng said. Game starts at 6 p.m.

headlines.title: San Francisco's historic Club Deluxe to reopen Thursday
headlines.description: A storied San Francisco jazz club that fell victim to the pandemic and a landlord dispute more than three years ago is set to reopen Thursday night under new ownership, bringing live music back to the heart of the Haight-Ashbury neighborhood. The Deluxe—originally known as Club Deluxe—has undergone a two-year restoration process. Its new owners hope the revived space will tap into San Francisco’s ongoing post-pandemic rebound and serve as a crucial hub for live entertainment and creativity. Christian Beaulieu and co-owner Jay Bordeleau are taking over a space that has been a neighborhood fixture since 1928. Over the last 98 years, the building has served as a creamery, a tailor shop, a gay disco, and a swingin’ jazz club. During the restoration process, workers uncovered various artifacts inside the walls highlighting the building’s past lives. Despite the extensive overhaul, the new owners recognize that the venue’s identity belongs to the community. “I feel it belongs more to the regulars and to the neighborhood than it does to me,” Bordeleau said. “I’m just allowed to be the steward of the space right now.” The reopening comes at a transitional time for the city’s music scene. While the owners look forward to welcoming long-term patrons, they are also aiming to attract a new generation of artists and listeners. According to Beaulieu, a new wave of jazz musicians is breathing life into the local scene by blending traditional sounds with modern genres. “A lot of people are coming to play the room for the first time,” Beaulieu said. “And then there’s also a cross-pollination of electronic music and jazz happening that’s very popular.” The owners are also eyeing San Francisco’s current artificial intelligence technology boom. They hope some of the wealth generated by the tech industry will find its way into the local arts scene, preserving the city’s cultural heritage. “The city is beautiful because people before us patronized the arts,” Bordeleau said, emphasizing that digital conveniences cannot replace the visceral experience of a live performance. “People want that in-life, real, tangible… you can’t DoorDash a band.”

headlines.title: San Jose plans massive World Cup viewing party as VTA breaks ridership records
headlines.description: World Cup fever is mounting in the Bay Area as local transit systems break ridership records and cities prepare for massive influxes of international soccer fans. San Jose officials announced plans to host the city’s largest World Cup viewing party yet at San Pedro Square, gearing up to welcome more than 25,000 fans for the upcoming group stage match between Mexico and South Korea on Thursday. The announcement follows a packed afternoon at the square, where large crowds gathered to watch England face off against Croatia. “It’s the World Cup, who wouldn’t wanna to be here to see people from all over the world and other cultures and watching great soccer?” said San Jose resident Chad Fegley. To manage the anticipated capacity crowds, the city is implementing strict security measures and expansive overflow protocols. Metal barriers have been installed at all entrances to San Pedro Square to prevent vehicle access, and municipal officials promised an increased presence of both police officers and private security guards. “We have San Pedro Square, and we have the street next to it open up,” said Tommy O’Hare, San Jose’s sports and special events director. “We’re also planning to open up Sharks Way and put up more screens there and have another additional overflow screen area as well.” O’Hare added that if the crowds continue to swell, the city has contingency plans to erect additional viewing screens within walking distance of the main square. The surge in local soccer enthusiasm is already testing regional infrastructure. More than 39,000 fans traveled by the Santa Clara Valley Transportation Authority (VTA) light rail system to attend the Jordan-Austria match at San Francisco Bay Area Stadium in Santa Clara. The single-day total set an all-time ridership record for VTA, eclipsing the agency’s previous record set during the Super Bowl. “We think it’s because we had a lot of visitors from other countries who ride trains more readily than they do here,” said VTA spokesperson Stacey Hendler Ross. While VTA successfully met its operational goal of clearing the stadium crowds in under two hours after the game ended, the transit agency is adjusting its strategy for upcoming matches based on early logistical hurdles. “On Saturday we learned that it’s not just enough to have ambassadors out there telling people where to go,” Ross said. “We also need more signs, so we put up big, tall signs that tell people go this way to Milpitas and that way to Mountain View.” Transit officials are also warning travelers that service will not run indefinitely. VTA plans to explicitly notify passengers that trains will cease operations three hours after matches conclude, advising fans not to rely on the light rail system late into the night.

headlines.title: Bay Area sales help set new record for California median home prices
headlines.description: Median home prices in California are at an all-time high with Bay Area sales helping set a new record for the state, the California Association of Realtors said. New numbers released by CAR show the median price for a single-family home in the state is $930,260 as of May 2026. The record is a 2.3% jump since April 2026, which is more than double the historical average increase between the two months. In the Bay Area, the region also set a record high last month with a median price for a single-family home at $1.45 million — a 3.6% jump from April. Three Bay Area counties saw median home prices over $2 million, the highest across the state. These include San Francisco, San Mateo and Santa Clara counties. San Francisco County’s median is currently $2.2 million, a 22.2% increase since May 2025. San Mateo County’s median is currently $2.4 million, a 9.1% increase since May 2025. Santa Clara County’s median is currently $2.1 million, a 2.5% decrease since May 2025. Not every Bay Area county saw an increase: Marin County saw 4% drop and Santa Clara County experienced a 3.3% dip. See CAR’s county breakdown of median home prices and the month-to-month change below. headlines.title: Audit highlights concerns regarding body cameras worn by Palo Alto PD officers
headlines.description: A newly released independent audit highlighted concerns about body camera compliance within the Palo Alto Police Department, as auditors found several incidents where cameras were not properly activated. “Body worn cameras are gamechangers when it comes to our auditing responsibilities and often are determinative in deciding whether or not the officers’ conduct was consistent with policy,” said Michael Gennaco with Oir Group. That role of body cameras is now at the center of an independent audit, reviewing officer misconduct complaints and use-of-force cases completed earlier 2026. “We found several situations in which for one reason or another the body worn camera did not depict all of the encounter or one of the officers on scene did not appropriately activate his or her body worn camera,” Gennaco said. The report recommended that the department strengthen training to ensure interactions are properly recorded. Palo Alto’s police chief says the incidents reviewed occurred largely during the first half of 2025. “In none of the cases were the officers found to have done anything inappropriate in the way they handled the call for service and more importantly, in none of the cases was there an absence in footage of the incident,” Chief James Reifschneider said. That’s because in instances where an individual officer’s camera didn’t work, other officer’s cameras caught the event. The department says officers now conduct camera checks before every shift and recently completed a transition to newer body cameras. “We think that this new camera that we have moved to will have better functionality, better battery reliability,” Reifschneider said. Auditors note they found no evidence officers intentionally failed to record encounters. Still, they say camera activation remains an area requiring continued attention. “Our recommendation is that the department should redouble its efforts in regards to training, maintenance and interventions to make sure that there is a record,” Gennaco said. The audit and the department’s response will ultimately be presented to the Palo Alto City Council later this year. Auditors say they’ll revisit the issue in future reviews to see whether compliance improves.

headlines.title: New art highlights San Francisco's American Indian Cultural District
headlines.description: This Saturday at San Francisco’s 16th Street BART Plaza, the city will celebrate the installation of two spheres painted by artists with tribal designs and images as part of the burgeoning American Indian Cultural District. The spheres were hand-painted by artists Maize Black and Felicia Gabaldon as part of an effort to tell the story of San Francisco’s American Indian community — as the city marks the 250th anniversary of its founding, which drastically impacted the area’s indigenous people who were already living in the region. “We truly believe this sort of art, this American Indian, this culturally-rooted art is something to elevate as a sense of pride and strengthen this neighborhood,” said Sharaya Souza, executive director of the American Indian Cultural District. For Black, who is of the Haudenosaunee Mohawk Confederacy and Gabaldon who is Choctaw, the project is an opportunity to feature American Indian art in a prominent location – the 16th Street BART Plaza, which is now designated by the city as part of the American Indian Cultural District. “This is a raven or a crow,” said Black as he painted a tribal design on the 3-foot orb. “The bird represents basically life.” The project is partially funded by the city as part of its Indigenize SF Initiative, a project aimed at highlighting American Indian culture through public art, cultural heritage and education programs. Souza said San Francisco’s indigenous communities have been left out of civic conversations far too long. “A lot of people don’t know that San Francisco is the third largest relocation city in the United States,” said Souza, standing in front of a large mural in the BART plaza depicting American Indian chief. “There are over 18,000 American Indians here in San Francisco.” The unveiling of the art spheres in the plaza, which will eventually number four, comes as the city marks the 250th anniversary of the arrival of the Anza expedition — a group of colonists led by Juan Bautista de Anza of the Spanish Empire who landed in what would become San Francisco on June 27, 1776, seven days before the signing of the Declaration of Independence at the other end of the continent. The anniversary is not a happy one for indigenous people whose ancestors were forced to work in the newly built Mission Dolores and the Presidio, stripped of their culture, and exposed to disease, which devastated the population. “There were several tribes, specifically the Ohlone, who were massacred,” said April McGill, executive director of the American Indian Cultural Center. “Several of our California Indian people were enslaved here, they were trafficked here, and those stories are the ones we’re not really talking about.” The artworks to be unveiled Saturday are part of a temporary installation but are part of an effort to create a permanent American Indian corridor through the Mission District, named for Mission Dolores, which the colonists built. “We obviously are still here, we still exist,” said Gabaldon as she painted the figure of a native woman on the sphere. “Which is why this is 250 years of resilience and honoring that.”

headlines.title: 2 men face murder charges in attacks on their estranged wives in San Jose
headlines.description: Editor’s note: This story contains details that some readers may find disturbing. Two men accused of killing their estranged wives in unrelated attacks over the past week in San Jose will be arraigned on Wednesday, prosecutors said. The most recent killing happened Monday in South San Jose, when a 49-year-old woman was found bludgeoned and stabbed to death at a home in the 5800 block of Snell Avenue. Officers responding to a welfare check about 12:45 a.m. found the woman’s husband, 55-year-old Shede Mao, walking out of the house. Inside they found his estranged wife’s body and a machete in a blood-spattered room, according to the Santa Clara County District Attorney’s Office. Police were alerted by a friend of the suspect, who said Mao had sent him a text that his wife “was all beat up” along with a photograph of her bloody body, prosecutors said. The woman, whose name has not been released, died at the scene. The second fatal domestic violence attack in recent days was reported on June 11, when police were called about 11:45 p.m. to an apartment on Descanso Drive in North San Jose. Officers found a woman who had been stabbed, allegedly by her estranged husband. The victim, 45-year-old Maria Liliana Vanegas Parra, was taken to a hospital where she later died of her injuries. The suspect, 46-year-old Pablo Andres Aguilera Mora, reportedly had a self-inflicted stab wound and was treated at a hospital, authorities said. Before the victim died, she told investigators that Aguilera Mora was her attacker, prosecutors said. San Jose Jun 12 San Jose Jun 15 The suspect previously had been arrested March 18 for allegedly stalking Vanegas Parra, slashing her tires and placing a tracker in her car. Aguilera Mora allegedly sent the victim more than 100 texts and voicemails and tried to get a job at the company where she worked. He had been released while those charges were pending, prosecutors said. “An Emergency Protection Order was granted and served. Less than three months later, the victim was dead,” the district attorney’s office said in a news release. Both men are set to be arraigned on Wednesday in Santa Clara County Superior Court. Nationally, while the numbers of most types of homicides have dramatically dropped, domestic violence deaths have remained at historically high levels, according to the district attorney’s office. The two deaths in June are the third and fourth domestic violence homicides so far this year in San Jose, prosecutors said. There were five domestic violence murders in Santa Clara County during all of 2025. A few days before the fatal attacks, a man shot his estranged girlfriend at a Kaiser Permanente medical office building in South San Jose. That shooting, on June 8, left a Watsonville man dead and a woman in critical condition. Investigators believe it was a murder-suicide attempt with evidence that the motive was domestic violence-related, the district attorney’s office said. “I am heartbroken, but sadness will never stop us from working with victims to get them and their children to safety,” District Attorney Jeff Rosen said. “Sadness will never stop us from making sure violent abusers face the strictest justice.” If you or someone you know is experiencing domestic violence, contact the National Domestic Violence Hotline by calling 1-800-799-SAFE (7233), visiting www.thehotline.org or texting LOVEIS to 22522.

headlines.title: California offers free admission to 30+ historic parks to celebrate 2 holidays
headlines.description: To honor two upcoming holidays, California announced Wednesday it is temporarily waiving the $50 fee for its historic park passport, which allows entry to more than 30 parks. California residents can secure their Historian Passport by registering on the Reserve California website and requesting a pass now through July 6. Once the pass is downloaded, passholders and three guests can access an unlimited number of historic parks for free from Friday through 2026. California Gov. Gavin Newsom said the promotion is to celebrate the 250th anniversary of the adoption of the Declaration of Independence on July 4, 1776, as well as the Juneteenth holiday, which commemorates the day around 250,000 enslaved people in Texas learned of their freedom years earlier. Newsom said the limited-time deal is meant to expand access to natural and cultural resources within the state while connecting visitors to places that preserve history. “I hope the free Historian Passport introduces more Californians to the state’s historic gems and sparks a curiosity, thirst for knowledge and sense of belonging that leads to many return visits,” California State Parks Director Armando Quintero said. The Antelope Valley Indian Museum is nestled at the foot of a butte and displays artifacts of the Great Basin peoples as well as some from the Channel Islands. Participating historic parks include: Anderson Marsh SHP Colonel Allensworth SHP Antelope Valley Indian Museum El Presidio de Santa Barbara SHP Bale Grist Mill SHP Empire Mine SHP Benicia Capitol SHP Folsom Powerhouse SHP Bidwell Mansion SHP Fort Ross SHP Bodie SHP Fort Tejon SHP California Citrus SHP Indian Grinding Rock SHP California Mining and Mineral Museum Jack London SHP La Purísima Mission SHP Los Encinos SHP Malakoff Diggins SHP Marshall Gold Discovery SHP Monterey SHP Olompali SHP Petaluma Adobe SHP Railtown 1897 SHP Robert Louis Stevenson SP San Juan Bautista SHP Shasta SHP Sonoma SHP State Indian Museum SHP Sutter’s Fort SHP Weaverville Joss House SHP Will Rogers SHP William B. Ide Adobe SHP Explore other opportunities to visit California historic sites for free by visiting the California State Parks website.

headlines.title: Richmond police investigate homicide
headlines.description: Richmond police were investigating a homicide Tuesday night, the department said. Police said the homicide happened along the 4500 block of Potrero Avenue. “At this time, there is no additional information available for release,” police said in a social media post Tuesday night. “More details will be provided as they become available and when it is appropriate to do so without compromising the investigation.”

headlines.title: Vallejo star LaRussell appears on America's Got Talent
headlines.description: Bay Area rapper LaRussell took the America’s Got Talent stage on Tuesday night’s episode. LaRussell brought local student musicians to perform onstage with him. He performed his track “I’m From The Bay,” which has become a community staple since its release at the beginning of this year. Judge Simon Cowell noticed the group’s individuality and energy. “Every one of you popped, but then together you became as one. That’s exactly the kind of act I was praying would come on this show,” Cowell said. LaRussell and his group proceeded to receive a “Yes” from all four of the judges, meaning they will move on in the competition. He hopes that this new platform will help spotlight local talent. Oakland Feb 9 Super Bowl Jan 28 Vallejo Oct 7, 2025 Vallejo rapper LaRussell performs sold-out concert in childhood backyard

headlines.title: Crews contain San Lorenzo house fire
headlines.description: No injuries were reported in a house fire in San Lorenzo, firefighters said. The blaze was reported Wednesday morning at a home on Via Arriba, near Kennedy Park. The Alameda County Fire Department said the fire started in a tree in the backyard and extended into the home. Two adults, four children and two animals were displaced as a result of the fire, Alameda County Fire Department said. The American Red Cross was called in to assist the family.

headlines.title: Bay Area Juneteenth celebrations: List of events and what to know
headlines.description: About Juneteenth in the Bay Area Around the Bay Area, Juneteenth events are scheduled to take place and celebrate Black culture, with a focus on music, art and food. Spanning Friday to Sunday, the holiday’s festivities will occur across the entire region, with headliner events taking place in Oakland, San Jose and San Francisco. Juneteenth commemorates the day Union troops of the Civil War made their way to Galveston, Texas, declaring the Emancipation Proclamation to free the nation’s final enslaved people. Observed annually on June 19, Juneteenth became an official federal holiday in 2021, signed into law by President Joe Biden. However, the Bay Area has celebrated the holiday since long before it was nationally recognized. The region’s oldest continuously running Juneteenth celebration is San Francisco’s Juneteenth Freedom Celebration, which had its 80th anniversary on Saturday, when it kicked off regional festivities. Taking place in the Fillmore District, the event filled eight city blocks with thousands of people, featuring free carnival rides and music concerts. San Francisco Jun 13 Oakland Jun 8 Juneteenth Jun 16, 2025 This weekend, the celebrations will continue, with major parades and festivals set to be hosted in Oakland, Antioch, Vallejo, San Francisco, Berkeley, San Jose and Palo Alto. All festivals will place a primary focus on promoting Black culture and entrepreneurship, featuring local Black artists and food vendors. On Friday, Downtown Oakland’s Hella Juneteenth Festival and San Jose’s Juneteenth Community Celebration will center around music. The Oakland event will be headlined by East Oakland native rapper and producer Ovrkast, who produced two tracks for Drake’s album “For All The Dogs Scary Hours Edition in 2023”. Pope Flynn, the Ghanaian-born master percussionist and vocalist for the Sweet Talks band, will lead a drumming workshop in San Jose. Other events, such as Juneteenth! at the Museum at the Oakland Mueseum of California on Friday, will highlight Black artists. Though the OMCA event will also include live music, the event’s main attraction is the museum’s special African-American exhibition “Mildred Howard: Poetics of Memory”, which links personal memory to broader history, showcasing the work of Bay Area artist Mildred Howard. Many of the Bay Area’s Juneteenth events are organized by community-led nonprofits and cultural organizations, though a few are city-sponsored, such as Antioch’s Juneteenth: A Freedom Celebration on Friday and East Palo Alto’s Welcome Black Weekend, which takes place Friday through Sunday. Even in the absence of a city-sponsored community event, most Bay Area city councils are working to recognize the holiday with gestures of Black solidarity. At Hayward City Hall, the city will raise the Pan African flags, while in Pleasant Hill, the City Council’s Diversity Commission will offer public remarks amidst its Juneteenth Celebration. Upcoming Juneteenth events in the Bay Area Antioch: Juneteenth Freedom Celebration June 19 from 4 p.m. to 8 p.m. Located at Williamson Ranch Park The event features live performances, music, food, activities and more. antiochca.gov/Juneteenth Berkeley: Juneteenth Festival June 21 from 11 a.m. to 7 p.m. Located at Alcatraz Avenue and Adeline Street Experience includes live performances, kid zones, food vendors, information sessions, merch and more. berkeleyjuneteenth.org East Palo Alto: Welcome Black Weekend Friday June 19 at 6 p.m. – Jubilee Concert and Dinner at EPACENTER Saturday June 20 at 12 p.m. – Festival in the Park at Bell Street Park Sunday June 21 at 1 p.m. – Gospel for Freedom Day at Community Church epacommunityarchive.org Menlo Park: Juneteenth Celebration Event on June 19 lasts from 5 p.m. to 8 p.m. at Belle Haven Community Campus Attendees will enjoy resources, vendors, activities, cultural entertainment and more. menlopark.gov Oakland: Hella Juneteenth Festival June 19 from 12 p.m. to 6 p.m. Located at Prescott Market West Oakland Festival includes DJs, vendors, dancing, games and more. Tickets range from $36 to $133, and are free for kids under 12-years-old. hellajuneteenth.com Oakland: Juneteenth at the Museum June 19 from 11 a.m. to 4 p.m. Celebrate with a vibrant day of culture, creativity and community. Guests can expect live performances, DJ sets, chefs, vendors and hands-on activities. Tickets cost $10 for all-inclusive and gallery access. museumca.org/juneteenth San Jose: Juneteenth Community Celebration Special programming on June 19 from 11 a.m. to 3 p.m. There will be a drumming workshop with Ghanaian-born performer, composer and educator Pope Flyne Other activities include photo ops, drum and friendship bracelet making. cdm.org San Francisco: 2026 Juneteenth Parade June 20 from 11 a.m. to 4 p.m. The parade will begin on Market and 2nd street to Embarcadero Plaza. Event includes the parade, performances and cultural exchanges. livablecity.org/juneteenth Vallejo: Juneteenth Parade and Festival Hosted on June 20 Parade at 9 a.m. on Tennessee Street and Sonoma Boulevard. Festival from 11 a.m. to 5 p.m. at Barbera Kondylis Waterfront Green. There will be live entertainment, food, merch, wellness info, activities and more. vallejojuneteenth.com
headlines.source: Adam Sutro | Bay City News and Sophia Taylor
